---
title: "Smart Amazon Product Query Assistant"
code-tools: false
description: "An AI-powered tool for enhancing the Amazon product search experience with natural language processing and machine learning."
date: 2026-04-19
categories: [NLP, Machine Learning, E-commerce, Python]
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    theme: lux
---

### The value of this project to me

- **Business analytics skills**:
    - translating a core marketing/finance metric (CLV) into approachable formulas and examples for a non-technical audience.
    - comparing historical vs. predictive methods and understanding the trade-offs between simplicity, accuracy, and cost.

- **Communication**:
    - structuring a technical topic into a 19-slide presentation with worked examples, suitable for stakeholders without a statistics background.

---

### Overview

Customer Lifetime Value (CLV) estimates the total net profit a business can expect from a customer over the duration of their relationship. This presentation covers why CLV matters, the major families of CLV calculation methods, and worked examples for each.

**Agenda**

1. Introduction to CLV
2. Historical CLV methods
3. Predictive CLV methods
4. Conclusion
5. Q&A

---

### Why CLV is hard

There is no single "correct" way to calculate CLV — every method involves assumptions and trade-offs.

- Even well-built models typically carry **15–25% error margins**.
- CLV is usually applied to **3–7 year** forecasting horizons, so small errors compound.
- Every approach faces the same dilemma: a CLV method can be at most two of **Simple**, **Accurate**, and **Inexpensive** — never all three.

---

### Historical CLV

Historical methods look backward at actual customer revenue/profit data.

#### ARPU / ARPA method

Average Revenue Per User (ARPU) or Average Revenue Per Account (ARPA) multiplies average revenue per customer by the expected customer lifespan.

**Worked example — Alice vs. Bob**

- Alice spends less per month but stays subscribed much longer than Bob.
- Even though Bob's monthly spend is higher, Alice's longer retention can make her CLV greater — illustrating why **retention matters as much as spend**.

#### Cohort method

Groups customers by acquisition period (e.g., signup month) and tracks their cumulative revenue over time.

- **Pros**: captures real behavioral trends within a cohort; easy to visualize as a revenue curve.
- **Limitations**: requires a long history to be reliable, and recent cohorts are inherently incomplete (their "future" hasn't happened yet).

---

### Predictive CLV

Predictive methods use statistical models to forecast future value rather than relying solely on historical averages.

- **Probabilistic models**: model purchase frequency and customer "alive" probability as random processes.
- **Pareto/NBD (Negative Binomial Distribution)**: a widely used probabilistic model that estimates how many future transactions a customer will make and the probability they are still an active customer, based on their past purchase recency and frequency.

#### Simple formula

$$
CLV = \text{Average Revenue per Customer} \times \text{Expected Customer Lifespan}
$$

#### Complete formula

A more complete formula incorporates profit margins, retention, and the time value of money:

$$
CLV = \left( \frac{\text{Revenue per Customer} \times \text{Gross Margin}}{1 - \text{Retention Rate} + \text{Discount Rate}} \right) - \text{Acquisition Cost}
$$

---

### Key variables

Four variables drive most CLV calculations:

- **Acquisition Cost**: cost to acquire a new customer (e.g., marketing spend per conversion).
- **Net Profit Margin**: the share of revenue retained as profit after costs.
- **Retention / Churn Rate**: the probability a customer continues (or stops) in the next period.
- **Discount Rate**: accounts for the time value of money when projecting future profits to present value.

---

### Conclusion

CLV ultimately helps answer three fundamental business questions:

1. **How much should we spend to acquire a customer?**
2. **Which customer segments are worth investing in retaining?**
3. **How sustainable is our current growth strategy?**

Choosing a CLV method is less about finding the "perfect" formula and more about matching the right balance of simplicity, accuracy, and cost to the decision at hand.

---

### References

- [For Entrepreneurs — Startup Killer: The Cost of Customer Acquisition](https://www.forentrepreneurs.com/){target="_blank"}
- [Custora — Customer Lifetime Value](https://www.custora.com/){target="_blank"}
- [Kaushik.net — Standard Metrics Revisited: Customer Lifetime Value](https://www.kaushik.net/avinash/){target="_blank"}
- [DataScience.com](https://www.datascience.com/){target="_blank"}
- [Above the Crowd — All Revenue Is Not Created Equal](https://abovethecrowd.com/){target="_blank"}
- [Wikipedia — Customer Lifetime Value](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Customer_lifetime_value){target="_blank"}
